draft.introduction: The Contendings of Horus and Seth (c. 1150 BCE, P. Chester Beatty I) narrates the legal dispute between Horus and Seth for the kingship of Egypt after Osiris’s death. It is a surprisingly irreverent, even comic, account of divine justice.

translation.text: The litigation of Horus and Seth, mysterious in their forms, mightiest of the princes and magnates who have ever been. A divine child sat before the Universal Lord, claiming the office of his father Osiris, the beautiful of appearances.
translation.original_text: wḏā(.t) n.t Ḥr ḥna Stš, sštā(.w) m jrw=sn, nḫt(.w) n sr.w wr.w jw.w ḏr.t

translator_notes.note: The Contendings is unique in Egyptian literature for its irreverent portrayal of the gods—they argue, deceive, and even assault each other. The Ennead’s indecisive tribunal has been read as satire of human bureaucracy, suggesting that Egyptians could approach their theology with humor.
